title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing — June 28, 2026
headline: Venezuela holds at 18.0 of 100 — earthquake aid accepted, relief slow and opaque; 12 false alarms stopped.
summary: June 28 is a confirmation-dominant cycle: 15 assessments, zero score changes, and 12 false-alarm signals stopped — a new single-night record. The lead story is Venezuela. Twin magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 earthquakes struck on June 24, killing more than 1,430 people and leaving about 68,900 families reporting someone missing. The benchmark scored the government's response, not the disaster itself. That response is mixed. The government accepted aid from 17 international flights carrying more than 1,600 rescuers from eight countries and the UN — the factor that kept Venezuela's score from falling and separates it from countries that block help entirely. But domestic relief was slow. Field medical workers said they did not know what supplies had arrived. About 14,000 troops and police limited access and required special permits. Venezuela holds at 18.0 of 100. The Lebanon-Israel ceasefire framework is now confirmed dead. Israel resumed strikes June 28, two days after the framework was signed and one day after Hezbollah rejected it. The speculative positive — that the framework might have led to peace — has not materialized. Lebanon holds at 17.2 of 100; Israel holds at 0 of 100. The US Commerce Department partially unblocked Anthropic's Mythos 5 model on June 26, allowing about 100 US companies and agencies to use it. This is a US government action taken on Anthropic — not Anthropic's own conduct. Anthropic holds at 59.1 of 100. In Somalia, the end-of-June date the IPC flagged as a possible famine threshold for Buur Hakaba district has now passed without a formal declaration. That formal declaration remains the scored change trigger. Somalia holds at 4.7 of 100. A rotation cache error was caught and corrected tonight: Venezuela's cache showed 4.4 — a score from the Maduro era — instead of the current authoritative score of 18.0, set on April 19 when the interim government took over. This is the largest cache correction in the benchmark's history.

topSignals.title: Venezuela 18.0 of 100: Twin Earthquakes Killed 1,430-Plus — Government Accepted Aid, But Relief Was Slow and Opaque
topSignals.whyItMatters: Venezuela accepted aid from 17 international rescue flights — the factor separating it from countries that block help. But domestic relief was slow, disorganized, and not transparent. The government's response is mixed. The score holds at 18.0 of 100.
topSignals.description: Venezuela holds at 18.0 of 100. Twin magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 earthquakes struck on June 24 — the strongest since 1900. More than 1,430 people are dead, about 68,900 families have reported someone missing, and roughly 60,000 buildings are destroyed. The benchmark scores the government's response to the disaster, not the disaster itself. Natural disasters are exogenous events — the government did not cause the earthquake. The response is the scored conduct. That record is mixed. On the positive side: 17 international aid flights landed carrying more than 1,600 rescuers from the United States, Cuba, Mexico, Colombia, Brazil, El Salvador, France, and the UN. A state of emergency was declared. A 200-million-dollar reconstruction fund was created. The government published the casualty count including the 68,900 missing figure. Accepting broad international aid is the key factor that separates Venezuela from countries at the absolute floor — countries like Yemen, where the governing authority forced the World Food Programme out of all 365 northern-area positions. On the negative side: domestic relief was slow and disjointed. Field medical workers said they did not know what supplies had arrived. About 14,000 troops and police limited access to affected zones, requiring special permits. Witnesses said officials took pictures rather than working. Two benchmark screening rules blocked a score change in either direction. The directionality rule prevented an upgrade: the entity appeared in the scan on a negative event, and accepting aid — while a meaningful positive — is not strong enough countervailing conduct to drive an upgrade from a negative surface. The double-count rule prevented a downgrade: the published score of 18.0 already accounts for Venezuela's weak institutional capacity and poor accountability. A capacity-limited response to the strongest earthquake in 126 years is severe, but it is consistent with what the Critical band already prices. Watch flags are now open on Action (ACT — relief responsiveness) and Accountability (ACC — aid-delivery transparency). The scored change triggers: documented deliberate aid obstruction or diversion would be negative own-conduct (downgrade); a demonstrably effective, transparent, and sustained relief effort would be positive own-conduct (upgrade).

evidence.quote: at 8 p.m. there were people alive down there, and they haven't bothered to rescue them
evidence.claim: PBS June 27: witnesses describe a slow and underprepared government response — residents dug through rubble by hand while officials arrived for photo opportunities; tensions peaked over what many Venezuelans viewed as an inadequate response.

evidence.quote: We don't know what arrived to Venezuela. We don't know where are those things that are coming.
evidence.claim: NPR / NBC June 27: field medical staff report aid-delivery opacity — supplies from international donors are untracked on arrival; access restricted by 14,000-plus troops and police requiring special permits.

Al Jazeera June 26: 17 international aid flights landed by June 27 carrying more than 1,600 rescuers from the US, Cuba, Mexico, Colombia, Brazil, El Salvador, France, and the UN; a 200-million-dollar reconstruction fund was created; state of emergency declared.

topSignals.title: Lebanon 17.2 of 100: Ceasefire Framework Confirmed Dead — Israel Resumed Strikes June 28
topSignals.whyItMatters: Israel resumed strikes on Lebanon June 28, ending the ceasefire framework signed two days earlier. The speculative positive — a framework that might have led to peace — never materialized. Lebanon holds at 17.2 of 100.
topSignals.description: Lebanon holds at 17.2 of 100. The US-brokered ceasefire framework signed June 26 by Lebanon, Israel, and the United States is now confirmed dead. Hezbollah rejected it June 27 as 'humiliation, disgrace, and surrender of sovereignty.' Israel resumed strikes on Lebanon June 28 following the framework's collapse. The sequence produced no score change for Lebanon at any stage. At the signing (June 26), the framework was a forward trigger — a condition that, if implemented, would produce positive own-conduct for Lebanon. Hezbollah's rejection (June 27) suspended the forward trigger. Israel's resumption of strikes (June 28) closes the trigger because the ceasefire that the forward trigger required has not occurred. The attribution rule also applies throughout: the ongoing civilian harm in Lebanon — more than 4,000 killed and 1.2 million displaced since March 2 — results from Israel's conduct, not Lebanon's. Lebanon is scored on what Lebanon does, not what is done to it. The revised forward triggers for Lebanon: a new ceasefire agreement and Lebanese Armed Forces (LAF) assuming sovereign control of southern Lebanese territory and reconstruction delivery would constitute positive own-conduct. New actions by Lebanon's government that facilitate or fail to prevent harm to its own population would be negative own-conduct.

evidence.claim: Hezbollah Secretary-General Qassem rejected the Rubio framework June 27 as 'humiliation, disgrace, and surrender of sovereignty'; Hezbollah pledged to continue fighting; Israel resumed strikes June 28.
Al Jazeera June 28: Israel resumed air strikes on Lebanon following the collapse of the Rubio framework; 4,000-plus people killed and 1.2 million displaced in Lebanon since March 2, 2026.

topSignals.title: Sudan 0 of 100: SAF Counterattack Opens Supply Road — RSF Ceasefire Offer Unimplemented; El Obeid Siege Day 16-Plus
topSignals.whyItMatters: The Sudanese army opened a supply road on June 28, but the RSF's offer to accept a four-country humanitarian ceasefire remains unimplemented. Sudan has been at the lowest possible score for over a year. New developments are forward triggers, not realized relief.
topSignals.description: Sudan holds at 0 of 100. The Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) launched a June 28 counterattack that reopened the Dalang-Kadugli road in South Kordofan — a route used for supply transport. This is an armed-operations move, not humanitarian own-conduct: opening a road through military action serves logistics for both sides in a civil war. The RSF accepted a humanitarian ceasefire proposal from a Quad of four countries (Sudan, Egypt, UAE, Saudi Arabia), but the SAF is skeptical and the proposal is unimplemented. Both developments are forward triggers — conditions that, if they develop into actual humanitarian access, would be scored own-conduct. Neither has produced realized civilian protection. El Obeid has been under RSF drone siege for 16-plus days. More than 500,000 civilians remain in the city. Famine has been declared in El-Fasher and Kadugli. The RSF's documented pattern of drone strikes followed by direct assault killed roughly 6,000 civilians in El-Fasher in three days in May 2025. Sudan is at the absolute floor; no lower score is possible.

evidence.claim: HRW June 26 action brief: RSF drone strikes Day 15-plus on power substations, fuel stations, displacement shelters, and a funeral gathering; more than 50 civilians killed; UNSC mass-atrocity warning (June 20) remains unactioned.
UNSC June 20 non-binding statement warned of imminent risk of mass atrocities; 500,000-plus civilians besieged in El Obeid; no enforcement resolution issued as of June 28.

topSignals.title: Somalia 4.7 of 100: End-of-June Famine Threshold Date Passed — No Formal Declaration Issued
topSignals.whyItMatters: The IPC's end-of-June target date for a possible famine declaration in Buur Hakaba has now passed without a formal declaration. A formal declaration is the scored change trigger. Somalia holds at 4.7 of 100. The famine risk window stays open through September.
topSignals.description: Somalia holds at 4.7 of 100. The International food security phase classification (IPC) had flagged the end of June as a threshold window for Buur Hakaba district, which has reached IPC AMN Phase 5 — extreme acute malnutrition, with a global acute malnutrition rate of 37.1 percent. One of three formal famine thresholds has been exceeded. But a formal IPC famine declaration requires all three thresholds to be met and an official IPC determination to be issued. As of June 28, neither the IPC nor the FSNAU has issued a formal famine declaration for Buur Hakaba or the neighboring district of Baidoa, which also reached IPC AMN Phase 5 in this cycle. The famine threshold date passing is not the scored trigger — the formal declaration is. Somalia's 4.7 score already prices the near-floor food-crisis conditions. The IPC famine risk projection extends through September 2026 for Bay, Bakool, and Gedo regions. About 6.7 million Somalis face high acute food insecurity. The World Food Programme is reaching about one in ten people who need food. Al-Shabaab continues to obstruct deliveries.

evidence.claim: IPC alert June 2026: Buur Hakaba and Baidoa both at IPC AMN Phase 5 (extreme acute malnutrition); IPC projects famine under worst-case scenario; no formal IPC Phase 5 famine declaration issued as of June 28.
FEWS NET June 2026: confirms Buur Hakaba at AMN Phase 5 (37.1% GAM); crisis persists across Bay region; famine declaration date-window has passed without formal issuance.
Crisis Group June 2026: Somalia barrels toward possible famine amid aid cuts; WFP reaching approximately one in ten people who need food; Al-Shabaab continues to obstruct food deliveries.

topSignals.title: Anthropic 59.1 of 100: Day 17 of Suspension — Mythos 5 Partially Unblocked for US Orgs; Fable 5 Still Suspended
topSignals.whyItMatters: The US government partially lifted its block on Anthropic's Mythos 5 model on June 26, allowing about 100 US companies and agencies to use it. This is the government's action, not Anthropic's own conduct — the score stays at 59.1 of 100, 0.9 points below the Established band.
topSignals.description: Anthropic holds at 59.1 of 100. Day 17 of the Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) export-control order that forced the global suspension of Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 — the first export controls ever applied to a commercially deployed AI model. On June 26, Commerce Secretary Lutnick sent a letter allowing Mythos 5 to be re-released to approximately 100 US companies and government agencies. Fable 5 remains fully suspended globally, including for Anthropic employees who are foreign nationals. The benchmark's attribution rule decides how to score this: the US government took the original action and took this partial reversal action. Both are US government conduct. A partial reversal of a government restriction on a company is not the company's own positive conduct — it is not Anthropic reducing suffering. No score change. Anthropic sits 0.9 points below the Functional-to-Established boundary (60.0 of 100). EU AI Act full applicability on August 2 — 35 days away — is the next structured compliance milestone for all AI labs.

evidence.claim: TechCrunch June 26: US government allows Mythos 5 re-release to more than 100 US companies and agencies; Fable 5 remains suspended; action is a Commerce Department export-control decision attributed to the US government.
Semafor June 27: US releases Anthropic Mythos 5 to some US companies; Fable 5 suspension continues; Day 17 of the export-control order.

topSignals.title: Israel 0 of 100: Resumed Lebanon Strikes June 28 Reinforce the Floor — No Lower Score Is Possible
topSignals.whyItMatters: Israel resumed air strikes on Lebanon June 28 after the ceasefire framework collapsed. The genocide finding issued June 23 by the UN Commission of Inquiry remains on the record. Israel is at the lowest possible score. No lower score exists.
topSignals.description: Israel holds at 0 of 100. Israel resumed air strikes on Lebanon on June 28, following Hezbollah's rejection of the Rubio ceasefire framework on June 27. The resumed strikes are own-conduct evidence reinforcing the floor. The Lebanon framework did not mandate Israeli withdrawal and is now effectively dead. In Gaza, the UN Commission of Inquiry (report A/HRC/62/CRP.2, June 23) found that Israel continues to commit genocide and other atrocity crimes by deliberately targeting Palestinian children. About 20,000 Palestinian children have been killed and 44,000 injured since October 2023. The ceasefire remains fragile — 936 fatalities since October 2025. The Kerem Shalom crossing is congested and the Zikim crossing has been closed for two weeks. The floor score of 0 of 100 reflects the most severe designation. Both developments — resumed Lebanon strikes and the genocide determination — reinforce the floor. No lower value is possible.

evidence.claim: Al Jazeera June 23 reporting on UN COI report A/HRC/62/CRP.2: Israel's deliberate targeting of children constitutes genocide and atrocity crimes; approximately 20,000 Palestinian children killed, 44,000 injured since October 2023.
UN News June 2026: Gaza ceasefire fragile — 936 fatalities since October 2025 ceasefire; one Palestinian child killed daily on average; Kerem Shalom congested; Zikim crossing closed two weeks.

boundaryWatch.trigger: EU AI Act full applicability on August 2 — 35 days away. Next US Commerce or BIS action on the Fable 5 export-control order. Mythos 5 partially unblocked for US orgs; Fable 5 still suspended.
boundaryWatch.note: Anthropic sits 0.9 points below the Functional-to-Established boundary at 60.0. Eighth consecutive cycle in this range. Day 17 of the export-control suspension. Mythos 5 partially unblocked June 26 for about 100 US companies and agencies — US government action on Anthropic, not Anthropic's own conduct. Fable 5 remains fully suspended. EU AI Act applies August 2.

boundaryWatch.trigger: NLRB review of the unfair-labor-practice charge filed after Apple closed its Towson, Maryland store — its first unionized store. An NLRB complaint or ruling is the conversion trigger. Congressional Labor Caucus called for investigation on June 27.
boundaryWatch.note: Apple sits 0.6 points below the Functional-to-Established boundary at 60.0. The 54-member Congressional Labor Caucus call for an NLRB investigation (June 27) is political pressure, not an NLRB complaint or ruling — pre-adjudication. Fourth boundary-watch cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: July natural Ebola peak window — now active. M23 territory overlap with WHO response corridors in North Kivu is the floor-crossing trigger if documented health-response obstruction occurs.
boundaryWatch.note: DRC at 2.3 Critical through cycle 15 of the Ebola emergency. 1,155 cases and 304 deaths as of June 25. Ituri province accounts for 1,054 of 1,155 cases. First EU-imported case in France (June 24). Contact tracing at 45 percent. July peak window is now active.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Formal IPC Phase 5 famine declaration covering Buur Hakaba or Baidoa. Both districts at IPC AMN Phase 5. End-of-June declaration window has passed without issuance; famine risk window extends through September 2026.
boundaryWatch.note: Somalia at 4.7 Critical. Buur Hakaba and Baidoa both at IPC AMN Phase 5 — extreme acute malnutrition. IPC projects famine under a worst-case scenario. The end-of-June threshold date has passed without a formal declaration. Famine risk window extends through September 2026. Fifth cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Coordinator-level Sahel-band calibration review. Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100) was reassessed in June on comparable UN-documented state-perpetration conduct. Mali's starting-point score of 12.5 is above the likely calibrated range.
boundaryWatch.note: Mali at 12.5 Critical on a starting-point seed score. Not assessed this cycle. The Sahel calibration flag is in its fifth consecutive day. The gap with Burkina Faso (6.3, reweighted June 1 on active-state-perpetration conduct) persists. Coordinator review covers Mali, Niger, and potentially Chad. No timeline set.

boundaryWatch.trigger: DOJ False Claims Act complaint (kickbacks and disability discrimination) and Barrows v. Humana class action (nH Predict AI claim denials). Either concluded finding confirms the Developing-band designation.
boundaryWatch.note: Humana at 35.2 Developing, 4.8 points below the Functional boundary. Seventh cycle post-downgrade. Not assessed this cycle. Neither the DOJ complaint nor the Barrows class action has a fixed hearing date.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Bengali Muslim and Rohingya expulsions documented in June 2026; detect-delete-deport digital surveillance continuing. Not assessed this cycle.
boundaryWatch.note: India at 15.6 Critical sits 0.6 points above the Critical floor through a fourteenth consecutive monitoring cycle. Not assessed tonight.

emergingRisks.risk: Venezuela's earthquake response is producing watch-flag evidence on two dimensions — Action (ACT) and Accountability (ACC). The domestic relief effort was slow and access-restricted. Field medical workers could not confirm what international supplies had arrived.
emergingRisks.detail: The score holds at 18.0 because the response is mixed — aid was accepted from eight countries and the UN, which blocks a downgrade. But documented aid diversion, deliberate obstruction, or suppression of casualty data in the coming days would be scored negative own-conduct and could trigger a downgrade below 18.0. Conversely, a transparent and sustained reconstruction effort would be scored positive own-conduct. Confidence: medium. Evidence still developing.

emergingRisks.risk: DRC Ebola is at the July natural peak window. 1,155 cases and 304 deaths as of June 25. Ituri province accounts for 91 percent of all cases. Contact tracing at 45 percent — half the rate needed to stop transmission. No approved vaccine for the Bundibugyo strain.
emergingRisks.detail: More than 70 health workers are infected, degrading response capacity. M23-controlled territory overlaps with WHO response corridors in North Kivu — documented obstruction by M23 would extend Rwanda's perpetrator attribution to a secondary pathway. The DRC government's contact-tracing failure (45 percent) remains the scored own-conduct driver. July peak is active.

emergingRisks.risk: Sudan's RSF drone campaign against El Obeid has entered Day 16-plus. The UN Security Council warned of imminent mass atrocities on June 20 — no enforcement resolution has followed. The RSF's documented pattern — drone strikes then direct assault — killed roughly 6,000 civilians in El-Fasher in three days in May 2025.
emergingRisks.detail: The SAF's June 28 counterattack reopened one road — this is an armed-operations development, not humanitarian relief. The RSF accepted a Quad ceasefire proposal but the SAF is skeptical and the proposal is unimplemented. More than 500,000 civilians remain in El Obeid with hospitals offline. Drone strikes have hit power substations, fuel stations, displacement shelters, and a funeral gathering.

emergingRisks.risk: Somalia now has two districts — Buur Hakaba and Baidoa — at IPC AMN Phase 5 extreme acute malnutrition. The end-of-June declaration window has passed without a formal IPC famine declaration. The risk window extends through September 2026.
emergingRisks.detail: WFP is reaching one in ten people who need food. Al-Shabaab continues to obstruct deliveries. The August lean-season peak will compound the situation. A formal IPC Phase 5 famine declaration for Buur Hakaba or Baidoa is the scored change trigger for Somalia's 4.7-of-100 score.

emergingRisks.risk: The Lebanon-Israel ceasefire framework is confirmed dead — Israel resumed strikes June 28. The war's civilian toll — more than 4,000 killed and 1.2 million displaced in Lebanon since March 2 — continues to be scored against Israel. The forward triggers for Lebanon's score have been reset.
emergingRisks.detail: A new ceasefire agreement AND Lebanese Armed Forces assuming sovereign control of southern Lebanese territory AND reconstruction delivery together would be scored positive own-conduct for Lebanon. Continued armed conflict is the scored negative own-conduct context for Israel — Israel sits at the absolute floor. The framework's collapse means the question of Lebanon's score now turns on whether Lebanon can establish sovereign control independently of a US-brokered deal.

emergingRisks.risk: The DOJ Olmstead memo signals a possible rollback of disability-integration law that has governed community-based care since 1999. The Equity and Accountability watch flags for the United States are formally open.
emergingRisks.detail: The Olmstead integration mandate requires states to serve people with disabilities in the most integrated setting appropriate. The DOJ Office of Legal Counsel memo questions that mandate. No enforcement action has occurred. The conversion trigger: formal DOJ enforcement withdrawal or a documented state institutionalization event with federal backing. The United States holds at 17.5 of 100 in the Critical band.

emergingRisks.risk: OpenAI faces simultaneous investigations from 42 state attorneys general and a Florida criminal probe. A concluded finding or settlement in either proceeding would move OpenAI's score from its current 27.5 of 100.
emergingRisks.detail: The 42-state subpoena covers data practices, handling of minors and seniors, model sycophancy, and internal safety policies. The Florida criminal investigation is tied to the FSU shooting. Both proceedings are ongoing. No fixed hearing dates have been set.

emergingRisks.risk: The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable on August 2 — 35 days away. General-purpose AI transparency obligations, Article 50 labeling, and whistleblower protections activate. xAI and OpenAI have not published compliance roadmaps as of this cycle.
emergingRisks.detail: A post-applicability non-compliance finding for general-purpose AI models constitutes a Tier-5 methodological finding and would move scores. Anthropic's compliance infrastructure is documented. The compliance gap between labs will become legally significant on August 2. Anthropic's Mythos 5 partial unblock does not affect its compliance posture under the EU AI Act.

emergingRisks.risk: Mali's enforced-disappearance and extrajudicial-killing pattern — documented by UN Special Procedures as potentially constituting crimes against humanity — remains unresolved in the Sahel calibration framework. The flag is in its fifth consecutive day.
emergingRisks.detail: The coordinator-level review covers Mali, Niger, and potentially Chad. Mali holds a starting-point score of 12.5 compared with Burkina Faso's reweighted 6.3. No timeline is set for the review. The gap has persisted through the entire June 2026 cycle series.

sectorAlerts.headline: Venezuela's earthquake response is the first Special Event Assessment in the June 2026 series — a four-rule §3e-bis compound determination confirmed the score at 18.0 of 100.
observations: Venezuela (18.0 of 100): the June 24 twin earthquakes are exogenous events — the benchmark scores the government's response, not the disaster. The Uganda natural-disaster-overlay precedent (June 6) applied.

Positive response conduct: 17 international aid flights from 8 countries and UN accepted; 1,600-plus rescuers admitted; 200-million-dollar reconstruction fund; state of emergency declared. This aid acceptance is what separates Venezuela from floor-cluster states that block assistance entirely.

Negative response conduct: domestic relief slow and disorganized; field medical staff unable to confirm what supplies arrived; 14,000-plus troops and police restricting access with special permit requirements.

The four-rule compound screen (directionality + double-count + attribution + pre-documentation) is the most complex single-entity §3e-bis determination in the June series. Score confirmed at 18.0; watch flags raised on Action (ACT) and Accountability (ACC).

A stale rotation cache (Venezuela showing 4.4 instead of authoritative 18.0) was corrected — the largest cache drift in the series at -13.6 points. The April 19 regime-change reset had not propagated to the rotation cache.

sectorAlerts.headline: Eight entities at or near the absolute floor; Israel resumed Lebanon strikes; SAF counterattack in Sudan; Lebanon framework confirmed dead.
observations: Sudan (0 of 100): El Obeid siege Day 16-plus. SAF counterattack June 28 reopened Dalang-Kadugli road — armed-operations move, not humanitarian. RSF accepted Quad ceasefire proposal; SAF skeptical; proposal unimplemented. Both are forward triggers.
Israel (0 of 100): resumed air strikes on Lebanon June 28. The Lebanon ceasefire framework did not mandate Israeli withdrawal and is now dead. UN COI genocide finding (June 23) remains on the record.
Yemen (0 of 100): Houthi-forced WFP termination of all 365 northern-area staff contracts continues as the dominant own-conduct fact across multiple cycles.
South Sudan (0 of 100): 7.8 million food insecure (56 percent of population); 140,000 displaced in Akobo; four counties at famine risk. Floor reinforced.

DRC (2.3 of 100): Ebola at 1,155 cases / 304 deaths — July peak window now active. Contact tracing at 45 percent. First EU-imported case in France (June 24). Own-conduct response failure remains the scored driver.
Iran (2.5 of 100): 784-plus executions year-to-date — a 37-year high. Execution rate unchanged post-Islamabad Memorandum period. Near-floor reinforced.
Somalia (4.7 of 100): IPC end-of-June famine threshold date passed without a formal declaration. Buur Hakaba and Baidoa both at IPC AMN Phase 5. Famine risk window extends through September 2026.
Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100): Sahel lean-season food crisis and second-largest IDP population in West Africa reinforce the near-floor. Sahel calibration flag enters its fifth day.

sectorAlerts.headline: The ceasefire framework arc is complete in 48 hours — signed June 26, rejected June 27, strikes resumed June 28; zero score changes across the entire arc.
observations: Lebanon (17.2 of 100): The Rubio framework signed June 26 created a forward trigger — an unimplemented condition that, if met, would produce scored positive own-conduct. Hezbollah's rejection June 27 suspended the trigger. Israel's resumption of strikes June 28 closes the trigger.

Israel (0 of 100): resumed Lebanon strikes are own-conduct reinforcing the floor. The framework's collapse means the Lebanon war continues under conditions that no longer have an active diplomatic resolution path.

The arc tests the benchmark's forward-trigger doctrine under adversarial conditions and confirms it: a signed agreement does not produce scored conduct; only implementation does. A rejected agreement that collapses within 48 hours produces no score change.

The revised forward triggers for Lebanon: a new ceasefire agreement AND LAF assuming sovereign control of southern Lebanese territory are the scored positive-conduct conditions. These conditions now require a new diplomatic process.

sectorAlerts.sector: AI Labs — Anthropic Day 17; Mythos 5 Partial Unblock
sectorAlerts.headline: US government partially unblocked Mythos 5 for US orgs on June 26 — scored against US, not Anthropic; Fable 5 still suspended; EU AI Act in 35 days.
observations: Anthropic (59.1 of 100, Functional band): Day 17 of the export-control order. Mythos 5 partially unblocked June 26 for approximately 100 US companies and agencies by a Lutnick letter from Commerce. Fable 5 remains fully suspended globally.

The attribution rule applied: the partial unblock is US government conduct on Anthropic — a government easing its own restriction. A partial reversal of a government restriction is not Anthropic's positive own-conduct. No score change.

EU AI Act full applicability August 2 — 35 days away. General-purpose AI transparency, Article 50 labeling, and whistleblower protections activate. Anthropic's compliance infrastructure is documented. xAI and OpenAI have not published compliance roadmaps.
The AI-lab score gap — Anthropic 59.1 of 100 at the top versus xAI 0 of 100 at the floor — is 59.1 points. This gap has held for eight consecutive cycles.

sectorAlerts.sector: Screening Discipline — Twelve False Positives (New Record)
sectorAlerts.headline: Twelve signals stopped in one night — the seventh consecutive night that the false-positive count increased; Venezuela's four-rule compound screen is the most complex determination in the June series.
observations: Venezuela's Special Event Assessment required a four-rule compound §3e-bis determination — the most complex single-entity screen in the June 2026 series, and the first to require a simultaneous directionality + double-count + attribution + pre-documentation analysis.
The Lebanon compound screen (directionality + attribution) fired for the second consecutive cycle, this time on the resumed-strikes signal rather than the framework-rejection signal.

The United States three-rule compound screen (stale-baseline-rejected + double-count + forward-trigger) fired for the second consecutive cycle, applied to the same set of DOJ Olmstead / deportation / detention evidence.

Pre-adjudication holds (Apple, UnitedHealth, Nigeria, Somalia) each screened identically to prior cycles. The rules are working as designed: investigations are not convictions; threshold dates are not declarations.
Seven-night false-positive trend: 1, 1, 3, 4, 8, 11, 12. The count has increased every single night since June 22. The record has been broken five times in seven nights.

methodologyNotes.name: Natural Disaster vs. Own-Conduct Scoring — The Venezuela Earthquake Protocol
methodologyNotes.description: The Uganda natural-disaster-overlay precedent (June 6, 2026) established the rule: natural disasters are exogenous events; the benchmark scores only the government's response. Venezuela's twin magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 earthquakes (June 24) mark the first application of this protocol to a Category 1 mass-casualty event in the June series. The scoring question was not 'did an earthquake happen?' but 'what did the government do about it?' The 1,430-plus deaths and 68,900 missing are disaster harm, not state conduct, and are not scored against the government. The government's response — aid accepted, but domestic delivery slow, opaque, and access-restricted — is the scored conduct. The four-rule §3e-bis compound screen (directionality + double-count + attribution + pre-documentation) is the most analytically intensive determination in the series. The outcome rests entirely on the response-scoring framework, not the disaster itself.

methodologyNotes.name: Aid Acceptance as the Floor Differentiator — Critical Band Calibration
methodologyNotes.description: The Venezuela determination formalizes a distinction that has been implicit in prior Critical-band assessments: a governing authority that accepts international humanitarian aid is in a different scoring category from one that blocks it. Yemen's Houthi authorities forced the World Food Programme out of all 365 northern-area staff positions — that is floor-level conduct. Venezuela admitted 17 flights and 1,600-plus rescuers from eight countries and the UN — that is what blocked a downgrade in this cycle. The domestic delivery failures (opacity, access restrictions, slow relief) are watch-flag-level evidence in a context where the government has not blocked aid at the source. The distinction: blocking access to aid is an active harm; being slow and opaque about aid already admitted is a capacity failure. The 18.0 of 100 Critical score already prices capacity failures and poor accountability. A new threshold event would require documented deliberate obstruction.

methodologyNotes.name: The Lebanon-Israel 48-Hour Arc — Forward-Trigger Doctrine Under Adversarial Conditions
methodologyNotes.description: The June 26-28 Lebanon-Israel arc tested the forward-trigger doctrine in its most compressed timeframe. A diplomatic agreement moved through three stages — creation, rejection, and collapse — in 48 hours. The benchmark produced a determinate outcome at each stage without modification to the doctrine: (1) framework signed June 26 — forward trigger logged, no score change; (2) Hezbollah rejection June 27 — forward trigger suspended, no score change; (3) Israel resumed strikes June 28 — forward trigger closed, no score change. The doctrine holds: a signed agreement does not produce scored own-conduct; only implementation does. A failed agreement produces no negative own-conduct for the party that signed in good faith. The doctrine was not modified or stretched across any of the three stages. Lebanon (17.2 of 100) and Israel (0 of 100) each held their published scores throughout the arc.

forwardTriggers.trigger: Documented deliberate aid obstruction, diversion or theft of relief supplies, or suppression of casualty data in the earthquake zone — scored negative own-conduct, downgrade trigger from 18.0 of 100. Conversely: demonstrably effective, transparent, and sustained relief and reconstruction delivery — scored positive own-conduct, upgrade trigger. ACT and ACC watch flags open.

A new ceasefire agreement AND Lebanese Armed Forces (LAF) assuming sovereign control of southern Lebanese territory AND reconstruction delivery together constitute scored positive own-conduct. The Rubio framework arc is closed as of June 28. A new diplomatic path is required for the positive trigger. Continued armed conflict is the scored negative-conduct context (attributed to Israel).
Full resolution-phase reassessment due. Conversion trigger: reversal of the June 8 codified-impunity law and release of detained union leaders.

July natural Bundibugyo Ebola peak window — now active. 1,155 cases and 304 deaths as of June 25. M23 territory overlap with WHO response corridors in North Kivu is the secondary trigger for attributing obstruction to Rwanda.
EU AI Act full applicability — general-purpose AI transparency obligations, Article 50 labeling, and whistleblower protections activate. No public compliance roadmap from xAI as of this cycle.
EU AI Act full applicability — same obligations apply. No public compliance roadmap from OpenAI as of this cycle.

EU AI Act full applicability — Anthropic's compliance infrastructure is documented. The applicability date tests whether it holds under enforcement conditions. Fable 5 remains suspended; Mythos 5 partially unblocked for US orgs.
August IPC lean-season update — IPC Phase 5 Borno trajectory and government response adequacy. Actual WFP food-assistance cuts and a realized Phase 5 famine declaration would be scored triggers.

Famine declaration window open through September 2026. Buur Hakaba and Baidoa both at IPC AMN Phase 5. End-of-June threshold date passed without formal issuance. Each new formal IPC Phase 5 famine declaration in Somalia is a scored change trigger.

RSF commencement of direct assault on El Obeid residential zones — the El-Fasher pattern of drone strikes then direct assault (6,000 civilians in three days, May 2025) is the documented comparator. Day 16-plus of drone campaign. SAF counterattack June 28 opened one road — a forward event, not realized humanitarian relief.

Formal DOJ withdrawal of Olmstead enforcement — the Equity (EQU) conversion trigger. Or: documented state re-institutionalization of a person with disabilities with federal backing — the Accountability (ACC) conversion trigger. Neither has occurred as of June 28.

Coordinator-level Sahel-band calibration review — Mali (12.5 of 100) versus reweighted peer Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100) on comparable UN-documented state-perpetration conduct. Flag in its fifth day; no timeline set.
42-state attorney general proceedings and Florida criminal investigation — no fixed hearing dates; any concluded finding, settlement, or criminal charge constitutes a scored change trigger.

Whether OCHA or IOM can establish alternative food-delivery mechanisms in northern Yemen following WFP's forced termination of all 365 staff contracts. Without food-agency presence, delivery to 22.3 million people is severely constrained.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: If Venezuela's government maintains aid-delivery opacity past the immediate disaster window — without documented diversion or obstruction — does sustained opacity on the Accountability dimension constitute new negative own-conduct sufficient to cross the 5-point threshold from 18.0 of 100?
dailyOpeningQuestion.tensionFraming: Venezuela accepted international aid — the key differentiator from floor states — while its domestic relief effort remained opaque and access-restricted.
